summary: Half-Step brilliance, fiery Shakedown>Crazy Fingers, epic Fantasy‑Hey Jude
blurb: The second set erupts with a high‑energy Shakedown Street into Crazy Fingers and a seamless Fantasy → Hey Jude transition. Jerry’s tone is beautiful on Half‑Step and his solo on Hey Jude shines, while Brent adds divine organ textures throughout.
review: Red Rocks on September 7, 1985 captured the Dead at a creative peak. The first set builds nicely with a sparkling Mississippi Half‑Step Uptown Toodeloo that showcases Jerry’s “Spanishy” tone and Brent’s tasteful keys, then rolls through solid cuts like New Minglewood Blues and Loser. The real fireworks ignite in the second set: Shakedown Street launches into a fiery Crazy Fingers jam, segueing into Samson & Delilah before the band launches into an unforgettable Uncle John’s Band → Playing In The Band → Space → Dear Mr. Fantasy → Hey Jude Reprise sequence that feels like pure Dead magic. Rhythm Devils’ drum‑duel and the expansive Drums/Space segment keep the momentum soaring, and the encore rounds it out with a raucous Johnny B. Goode and a heartfelt Baby Blue. Across dozens of tapes the performance is consistently praised for its tight ensemble work, vibrant improvisation, and Brent’s spontaneous Hey Jude coda, making this show a standout entry in the mid‑80s era.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The majority of recordings (10 out of 13) are marked positive, highlighting the show’s high energy, standout jams and strong musicianship. Only a few mixed notes mention vocal sag on specific songs; no consistent negative criticism about overall performance appears. This strong, uniform praise justifies a sizable sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Crisp soundboard capture with excellent instrument balance, minimal technical issues, and the most complete representation of the performance.

key_highlights: Frozen Logger goofy opener that sets a playful tone (though not on the official setlist)
Shakedown Street into Crazy Fingers jam ignites the second‑set energy
Epic Fantasy → Hey Jude transition with Brent’s organ soaring
Rhythm Devils drum duel and expansive Drums/Space segment
song_highlights: It's All Over Now Baby Blue

band_performance.Jerry: Beautiful, Spanish‑tinged tone and juicy solos on Half‑Step and Hey Jude; occasional vocal fatigue noted later in the set but overall a strong showing.
band_performance.Phil: Provides solid bass foundation throughout; his playful kazoo/horn moment during the Frozen Logger adds fun texture.
band_performance.Bob: Vocals are mixed – solid on classic numbers like One More Saturday Night, but some reviewers note weaker delivery on frozen‑logger moments; still contributes energetic rhythm guitar work.
band_performance.Brent: Spontaneous Hey Jude coda and divine organ work on Shakedown Street and Fantasy; praised as a creative force adding rich textures.
band_performance.Mickey: Great beam work and highlighted drums/space segment; Rhythm Devils jam is cited as one of the finest post‑’83 outings.
band_performance.Billy: Tight, supportive drumming that locks in with Mickey; drives the high‑energy flow especially during Crazy Fingers and the drum duel.

must_listen_sequences: It's All Over Now Baby Blue
